text: audio plays a huge role in how a film feels and sound design is a big part of that it helps shape the flow and pacing of a film adds depth to the edit and connects the viewer to what's happening on screen in ways they might not even realize it's what makes a scene feel immersive whether through subtle Ambience or dramatic sound effects as filmmakers it's easy to fall into the Trap obsessing with visuals but sometimes in that process we forget that audio is just as important just like lighting or color grading sets a visual mood sound design does the same but on a deeper level but where do we start the vocals are usually the most important sound in a video which can be dialogue or voice over carrying the story and information which you don't want to get lost in the mix by being too quiet or overpowered by other elements next we have the background music which is super important to help set the foundation of the pacing and overall mood of the video and I personally sometimes like to find the music before I even start shooting and while this approach might not work for every type of video this can help me visualize the overall feel and energy of the project and even influence how I decide to shoot and once those are in place I'll start focusing on the sound design elements beginning with Ambiance or environmental sounds Ambiance is often the first layer of sound design that I add to the timeline and it's the background noise used to help establish the environment the scene takes place in and make your scene feel alive and immersive it shouldn't be overpowering just be there in the background depending on your scene it could be things like wind and subtle C noise or lapping water and some burn Shing they're subtle but crucial for grounding your audience in the setting then we layer in our sound effects which I like to group into two main categories first there's sounds that comes from the actual environment like a corridor closing footsteps or just anything in your scene that you feel like would make a sound just like Ambience these don't necessarily draw too much attention into themselves but they are essential for creating a solid foundation Foley also falls under this category which is when you record the sounds yourself specifically for the project to sync to the onscreen actions then we have the creative sound effects which are added as a creative Choice rather than realism it can be anything from whooshes impacts and risers or just any other sound you can think of to elevate your scene or story wooes are incredibly be versatile and I use them to make Cuts Transitions and movements feel more intentional and risers are a powerful tool for building anticipation and suspense and the most common uses of risers is building up to a

can think of to elevate your scene or story wooes are incredibly be versatile and I use them to make Cuts Transitions and movements feel more intentional and risers are a powerful tool for building anticipation and suspense and the most common uses of risers is building up to a reveal or smoothing out the cut to a completely different environment or energy level which leads me to the next point the absence of sound is just as important as the presence of it just like blending fast based handle movements with static shots gives an eded room to breathe we can apply the same principle to sound assign instead of filling every second with layers of sound silence or just a quieter moment builds contrast and make the returning of the sound or music even more impactful the fun thing about sun sign is that it doesn't have to be literal just because you see something on screen doesn't mean you have to match it with the exact same sound it make in real life instead think about how you want the audience to feel and experiment with different sounds to reinforce that emotion I can make it sound like it rains outside or like someone's playing music outside my door when in reality I don't even have neighbors also if I want the Tuk Tuk to sound like a sports car I can before we jump into some of my favorite sound design techniques I just want to talk about the place I always go to when I'm searching for high quality sound effects which is artist which was also kind enough to sponsor this video they are an all-in-one website for all kinds of assets you might need for your film making process and I've used their music and sound effects since the start and their Sound Library is seemingly endless so whatever sound you're looking for there's a good chance you will not find this one but multiple options that could fit your scene one of my favorite additions to their music library especially when it comes to sound design is that many songs include stems that gives you as an editor the freedom to build a perfect underscore for your story if there's some distracting instrument in a song you can just mute that track and make the perfect song to fit your video apart from their extensive sound effects and music they have stock footage video templates an AI voiceover generator lots and they are constantly adding new things for us creators to use they have several different subscription plans to fit your needs all cleared for commercial use so if you want to dive more into sound assign or anything else to make your videos come to life I have a link in the description that will give you two additional months if you decide to sign up thank you artlist for supporting the channel let's move on to some of my favorite

dive more into sound assign or anything else to make your videos come to life I have a link in the description that will give you two additional months if you decide to sign up thank you artlist for supporting the channel let's move on to some of my favorite sound design techniques one of the most important sound design techniques to make your sound more realistic is to use panning to position sounds either to the left speaker or to the right speaker and here's the shot of US cycling in Japan without any panning and with panning subtle panning can make your Sunscape sound more Dynamic and realistic a great way to smooth out cuts and make them feel more seamless when transitioning from scene to scene is to use a j cut that means that the audio from the next scene start playing before the visual cut happens you do that by extending the audio to the left and gradually fade it in before the clip appears being able to hear the sound before the cut really helps to hide the cut and make it feel more motivated and to also let the audio from the previous scene linger as the visuals change is called an L cut these techniques can make edits flow a bit better even if the cut is to something completely different here's the audio timeline from my Japan video and here you can see all the j cuts and L cuts of the blue Ambience tracks fading in and out there are a few ways you can enhance sound effects and tweak them to make them fit the environment better in most editing programs there's a bunch of built-in audio effects to choose from but there are three that I tend to use in every single project the first one is reeverb which is used to create a sense of space or atmosphere making sounds feel like they actually belong in the environment for example a small room should only have a tiny amount of Reverb while a large church should have a much bigger and Echo sound the size of the space determines how much Reverb you need or if a certain sound effect sounds a bit dry or abrupt you can use Reverb to make the sound feel larger and more fluid Reverb can also help push sounds further into the background and right now this bird sounds really weird and up close but the more Reverb you add the more it sounds like the birds are coming from a distance EQ is also something I use in every single project which you can use to both decrease or increase different audio frequencies it's a great tool for enhancing voices but you can also use it to create room for different sounds for example if I have a music track and a voice over track that uses a lot of the same frequencies I could remove some of those frequencies

different audio frequencies it's a great tool for enhancing voices but you can also use it to create room for different sounds for example if I have a music track and a voice over track that uses a lot of the same frequencies I could remove some of those frequencies of the voice from the music to make the voice more prominent another use of EQ is to create a high pass or low pass filter highpass will cut out all the low frequencies of your audio and low pass will cut out all the high frequencies you can use this effect to mimic for example different speakers like this phone speaker or you can make audio sound muffled or like it's underwater and here's our normal wave sound effect sounds like when I add the low pass filter now we've come to the last step of the process which is the mixing where you want to make sure that nothing's too loud or too quiet and that the levels are consistent throughout the video after you've added a bunch of sounds to the timeline it's easy for the audio levels to start creeping up into the Reds and if the audio is clipping it will not sound very nice once you export the video but let's say you're happy with the overall mix but the levels are still too high then you can just lower the moner volume which in this case is bus one to make sure nothing is peing a quick tip if you use Da Vinci is in the deliver page under audio and audio normalization we have the option to normalize our entire mix to meet different audio loudness standards so I usually take this to prevent additional audio compressions from us before you press publish it's a good idea to listen to your film in different speakers to make sure everything sounds like it should sound design can be a super powerful tool for creating more immersive videos and it can be a little bit terrifying when you see someone's timeline and it's like hundreds of audio tracks stacked on top of each other and also since sound design can be so abstract you can basically do whatever you want but by just watching your Clips figuring out what sounds are present in those specific environments and What feelings you want the audience to get when they watch it then you've already come a long way and sometimes you don't need more than one or two layers of sound and I know sound isn't the most fun topic to talk about but if you made it this far in the video that means you're still awake which is great and I hope this video has inspired you a little bit to experiment more with sound design since it's such a crucial part of the viewing experience and don't forget the two additional months
